content.problemStatement:
You are given NNN integer sequences A1,A2,…,ANA_{1}, A_{2}, \ldots, A_{N}A1​,A2​,…,AN​.

Each of these sequences contains NNN elements.

You should pick NNN elements, one from each sequence; let the element picked from sequence AiA_{i}Ai​ be denoted by EiE_{i}Ei​.

For each i  (2≤i≤N)i \; (2 \leq i \leq N)i(2≤i≤N), the condition
[
Ei>Ei−1E_{i} > E_{i-1}Ei​>Ei−1​
]
must hold.

Compute the maximum possible value of
[
E1+E2+⋯+EN.E_{1} + E_{2} + \cdots + E_{N}.E1​+E2​+⋯+EN​.
]

If it is impossible to pick such elements E1,E2,…,ENE_{1}, E_{2}, \ldots, E_{N}E1​,E2​,…,EN​, print −1-1−1 instead.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N. Then N lines follow, each containing N space‑separated integers A[i][j] (1 ≤ i, j ≤ N).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the maximum possible sum E1+E2+⋯+EN satisfying E_i > E_{i-1} for all i≥2. If no such selection exists, print -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Pick 3 from the first row, 6 from the second row and 9 from the third row. The sum is 3+6+9=18, which is maximal.
Pick 2 from the first row and 4 from the second row. The sum is 2+4=6.
Only one element can be chosen, so the answer is 1.

solution.approach: We process the rows one by one. For each element A[i][j] in row i we keep dp[i][j] = maximum sum achievable when we pick this element as E_i and the sequence up to row i is strictly increasing. The transition is dp[i][j] = max_{k where A[i][j] > A[i-1][k]} (dp[i-1][k] + A[i][j]). The answer for a test case is max_j dp[N-1][j]. The straightforward implementation checks every pair of elements from two consecutive rows, resulting in O(N^3) time overall (N rows, N choices per row, and N comparisons for each transition). This fits within the limits because N ≤ 700, giving at most about 3.4·10^8 elementary operations, which runs in time in compiled languages. The DP can be reduced to O(N^2) by sorting each row and keeping prefix maximums, but the simple O(N^3) version is sufficient for the given constraints. Memory can be reduced to two rows, but a full N×N table is used for clarity. All sums are stored in 64‑bit integers to avoid overflow.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^3) per test case (N rows, N elements per row, and O(N) work for each transition). The total over all test cases is O(∑ N^3) ≤ O(700^3) ≈ 3.4·10^8 operations.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) auxiliary DP table (≈ 700·700·8 bytes ≈ 4 MB) or O(N) with row‑wise optimisation.
